title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several key items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, and directed staff to develop enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations at $100/day. The committee also approved construction agreements for the Military Road project and consulting services for upcoming street projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permit

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, covering over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. This 2029 project may be moved up to 2028. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road settling over sewer trenches, with ongoing warranty monitoring required.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal, mailbox and sign repairs, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Significant dewatering was required along the Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week due to flooding. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance and changeover from winter to summer operations was detailed.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration issues, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. Staff identified sump pump discharges into sanitary sewers as a major contributor. The committee discussed the upcoming INI study by In-Pipe Technologies to identify infiltration sources. Kennedy Park Farmers Market received positive feedback with no parking or traffic issues reported.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility's value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The committee acknowledged the report unanimously.

The committee recommended approval of a League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supporting long-term transportation funding solutions. Michael noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and relies on general tax dollars to subsidize transportation costs. The motion passed unanimously.

The committee awarded the 2026 recycled asphalt crushing project to King Gravel at $4.23/ton, with an estimated total of $126,900 for 30,000 tons. Michael demonstrated this saves approximately $11 per cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, totaling about $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee discussed enforcement of ordinance violations for clear water discharges into sanitary sewers. After debate about appropriate penalty levels, the committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines of $100 per day for violations, distinguishing between unknowing first-time offenders who receive warnings and willful repeat violators who face fines. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction, with Weston's share of approximately $94,860 for water and sanitary sewer work. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael recommended MSA for the Alderson/Jelinek roundabout project at $37,315 and Clark Dietz for the Bladel Avenue project at $73,730, citing preference for experienced full-time staff over interns. Both recommendations were approved unanimously.

actionItems: Approved resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding - forward to village board
Awarded recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900 estimated total
Staff to draft enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations at $100/day penalty
Approved Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for approximately $94,860
Approved MSA for Alderson/Jelinek construction services ($37,315) and Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue ($73,730)
Special assessment meeting for Bladel Avenue scheduled for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Water main flushing notices to be mailed, flushing to begin next week
Staff to modify Radkey Avenue utility agreement to include 2029 deadline for reconstruction
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs for next meeting
Staff to provide street lighting update for future meeting

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work product transmittals, educational presentations and reports
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: The commission elected Trustee Erling as Chair, Cushing as Vice Chair, and Renee as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously by voice vote. There was brief procedural confusion about seconding the Vice Chair nomination, which was resolved when Jackson provided the second.
Minutes from April 21st were approved unanimously. Motion was seconded by Renee.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) presented the grant request for the annual Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting 11,000-12,000 attendees last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commissioner Hodal questioned room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year despite last year's $5,000 grant. The commission requested better tracking of hotel room usage for future applications. Motion to approve $5,000 grant passed unanimously, seconded by Jackson.

Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar. She described meeting the candidates, noting that Anastasia won the competition. The candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese, and Renee noted this was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow Balloonfest representatives to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with the commission taking steps to protect municipal water supplies and advance public safety infrastructure.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff updates

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to address a 36-inch gas pipeline running through the property, which allowed the building to be placed on higher ground and reduced fill costs. The facility is designed for 50-60 officer capacity for future growth, up from current staffing of 40 officers. Discussion included placement of an alternate storage shed to allow for future expansion northward. A motion was made to recommend approval to the board of trustees subject to staff conditions 2-5. The motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a twice-annual inspection program for salt storage facilities within wellhead protection areas - a warm weather inspection focusing on structural integrity and a winter inspection focusing on operations. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and the commission agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Commissioner Lumper praised the program as well put together. A motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Minutes from the April 13, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously with no discussion.

Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion followed about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into the comprehensive plan process.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via remote connection. He asked the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to ensure complaints don't get lost.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility recommended for approval to Board of Trustees subject to staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation with reporting to Plan Commission and Public Works Committee
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
First joint steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invite for steering committee meeting
Electric vehicle charging stations noted as future agenda item for fall
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an educational orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No policy votes were taken; the meeting focused on introducing department missions, services, budgets, and organizational structures to committee members.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote attendance confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - no items for this meeting
Review of standing committee ordinances, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Lance Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by VSO Erin Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with all in favor.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee ordinances and jurisdiction
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's structure under ordinance section 2.04 sub 5, including oversight of Health Department, Social Services, and Veteran Services. He outlined relationships with affiliated boards including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Board of Health, North Central Healthcare Board, and ADRC Board. Chair Boots noted the committee will focus on the new strategic plan over the next two years.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. She highlighted 1,200+ licenses overseen, public health vending machines with free resources including tick kits, the Nurse Family Partnership program with first graduates, and a new FTE position for homeless system response. The department has 37.6 FTE and about $5 million budget with approximately half from tax levy. Administrator Leonard noted the department's diverse funding including grants and public charges for services.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of absent Director Christa, describing the department's approximately 140 FTE staff covering child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the Family Keys program piloting housing stability services, noting it was substantially changed based on board feedback and has been very successful addressing housing instability root causes. The department's total 2026 budget exceeds $22 million with about $7 million from tax levy.

VSO Erin Galindo reported the office now has 3.0 FTE and had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900 the previous year. He noted $44 million annually comes into the county through veteran benefits, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include surviving spouse DIC benefits (worth $1,800-2,400/month), housing stability for veterans through HUD-VASH partnerships, and suicide prevention efforts. Galindo reported Marathon County had 30 suicides in 2025, with 7 being veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have its own VSO and that his office can help any veteran anywhere.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) as a one-stop shop for aging and disability services. He described significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs rising 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat. The ADRC hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024, demonstrating fiscal efficiency. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135. The organization has about 60 FTEs and relies on 385-400 volunteers, particularly for the Meals on Wheels program.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before the committee
Mike Ray (ADRC) to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)
Committee members encouraged to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with agenda suggestions for future meetings

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and appointed two new members to the CLIPS committee.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees called to order, roll call, and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of minutes from previous meetings
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - license renewals and vouchers
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bikes
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.body: Staff presented a proposal to increase park and recreation impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Both the plan commission and park and recreation committee recommended the increase. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

During public comment, Jim Pitel questioned why the engineering technician position hasn't been filled despite board approval. Public Works Director Michael Dolan explained the position has been advertised multiple times but the specific skill set required is scarce in central Wisconsin, noting even consulting firms are pulling from southeast Wisconsin offices. The board will consider approving two outside engineering consultant contracts totaling approximately $108,000 for project oversight.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates a regional agreement where SAFER and surrounding municipalities (Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Cloverland, Riverside) will recognize each other's fire inspections of food trucks through a sticker system, eliminating duplicate inspections. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously with motion by Lope, second by Zagami.

The board debated amendments to the remote attendance policy. Trustee Beck moved to amend the policy to strike sentences requiring health/safety/welfare justification for remote attendance, making the policy more broad. The amendment passed 5-2 with Hermling and Lope Sorrell opposed. The original motion as amended then passed 5-2 with the same dissenting votes. The final policy requires 12-hour advance notice, camera on, and prohibits remote participation in closed sessions.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, passed unanimously. The board discussed having future applicant resumes included in board packets. Openings remain on Parks, Finance/HR (two positions), and CLIPS (one position).

Scofield Coin and Hobby LLC applied for secondhand article and jewelry dealer licenses at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained these licenses involve buying/selling precious metals with large dollar amounts and require reporting to a police-accessible database to track potentially stolen merchandise. The motion was amended to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit, then passed unanimously.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market's relocation to Kennedy Park after losing the Camp Phillips/Scofield Avenue location to development. Trustee Beck raised concerns about parking on grass being a safety issue. Renee (market organizer) explained the market grew from 25 stalls at the original location to 42 at Camp Phillips to 53 at Kennedy Park. The board acknowledged if no suitable location can be found, the market may need to be suspended. Renee confirmed the market operates at a profit through vendor fees, grants, and reimbursement programs.

Public Works Director Dolan reported on the speed limit issue from a prior board meeting. The engineering study showed it should be 35 mph, but the 85th percentile speed study showed traffic at 45 mph. He will collect additional data after construction ends and bring the matter back to the board for decision.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 5202 Street spoke during public comment, criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He questioned why committee appointments are discussed privately before meetings, citing Lisa Beck being introduced at a SAFER meeting before official appointment. He also criticized the lack of detailed information on Kennedy Park in the meeting packet and called for greater administrator accountability.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective upon ordinance passage
Comprehensive Plan Steering Committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 6:00 PM
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspection assistance
Speed limit study for Weston Avenue to be completed and brought back to board
Future committee/commission applicant resumes to be included in board packets
Scofield Coin and Hobby license contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Remote attendance policy amendments to be documented with strikethrough changes

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the Executive Committee, and unanimously passed a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris following severe tornado and flooding damage in the Ringle area. The board suspended rules to address the emergency tipping fee measure directly from the Solid Waste Management Board.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Roll call (technical difficulties with meeting manager)
Committee appointments discussion and remarks from Chair
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Modification to landfill tipping fees

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks thanking staff and Vice Chair Boots for assistance in developing committee appointments, noting 34% of the board is in its first or second term. He emphasized that committee appointments were guided by goals including increasing perspective representation and supervisor development. The motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims, was carried but not unanimous. Supervisor Dobeck noted she was marked absent but was not given the option to vote.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Supervisor Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's children and elders. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.
Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was carried but not unanimous with no discussion.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was carried but not unanimous with no discussion.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Modification to Landfill Tipping Fees
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented the resolution addressing debris from severe tornado and flooding damage, particularly in Ringle. The proposal reduces tipping fees from $65/ton standard rate to approximately $36.94/ton by removing the state fee ($13.10), town of Ringle fee ($2.33), and county portion. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak and requested the county waive fees entirely for affected residents. Administrator Leonard mentioned potential partnership with Community Foundation for unreimbursed costs. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to bring the resolution directly from Solid Waste Management Board, then voted unanimously to approve the resolution with motion by Supervisor Arstead and second by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County waive fees entirely for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are waiving their fees and that private businesses have donated significantly to cleanup efforts. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes need demolition, requesting fee relief for underinsured homeowners and noting some insurance adjusters have not yet arrived at affected properties.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee as at-large members
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on mechanism for reimbursing unreimbursed insurance costs for disaster victims
Solid Waste Management Board directed to develop clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Staff to track disaster-related waste through certification forms from haulers

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and passed an amended resolution to waive only septic system reconnect fees (not other zoning or pouts fees) for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (conservancy recreation to general agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition - Town of Green Valley
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for or against. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition - Town of McMillan
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the rezoning from conservancy recreation to general agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature, and Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, with the rezoning needed to make the home a conforming use rather than non-conforming. He confirmed no natural areas would be lost. Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather damage
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for tornado-damaged properties in Ringle, Kronenwetter, Easton, and Weston. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Leur asked about tracking costs. Supervisor Drebeck raised concerns about fairness to individuals hit by isolated storms without disaster declarations. Eric Vogel asked if insurance would cover fees. After debate, Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees ($160) for septic systems, not other zoning or pouts fees. The amendment passed. The amended resolution to forward to the county board passed, though not unanimously.

Director David Hagenbusher presented an overview of solid waste operations, explaining the landfill's 45-year history, current tipping fee structure, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy generating royalties since 2023, and upcoming challenges including a $6 million cell construction project and required leachate treatment system by 2032 due to PFAS concerns. He noted April hauling costs were nearly $90,000 due to significant rainfall.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie who had an emergency. He explained the department serves both city and county with 44 full-time and 120 seasonal employees, manages 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest, and 37 city parks. He noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefelt announced that Tom is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as his replacement.

actionItems: Rezoning for James Offer and David Tract (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential in Town of Green Valley) approved and forwarded to County Board
Rezoning for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz (conservancy recreation to general agriculture in Town of McMillan) approved and forwarded to County Board
Amended resolution to waive only septic system reconnect fees ($160) for tornado-damaged properties forwarded to County Board for approval
Staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month due to the chair's absence, and approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Plover River. The committee also received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and discussed graffiti issues on the Oaklair River Trail.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved. A motion was made and seconded with no discussion. The motion carried unanimously.

Director Sean Oster Bring presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park project from its inception through the May 9th ribbon cutting. He explained that the Friends of Kennedy Park is fundraising for lights on the upper field, currently at approximately $84-85,000 toward a $117,000 goal. Committee member Scott noted the project has no firm timeline and could take 10-20 years. Oster Bring clarified that the village had approximately $7 million earmarked for infrastructure but those items were pulled from the capital improvement plan pending the master plan's development. Youth Baseball contributed $8,700 for bases, mats, mounds, and screens for the new fields.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos and a PDF showing the location of fallen trees removed from the river and the wood mat installation over gas pipelines. The mats were provided by TC Energy (formerly ATC/ANR Pipeline) and are now owned by the village, with extras potentially available for another trail project.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The item was deferred to next month because committee member Katrina, who requested the item, was absent due to an unforeseen issue. One member of the public, Andy (6504 [address incomplete]), spoke against allowing dogs in the pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' The committee noted his objection would be recorded.

Director Oster Bring explained challenges in removing graffiti from concrete and benches on the trail, noting the pressure washer cannot access the area due to steep ditches and narrow bridges. Staff discussed potential solutions including anti-vandalism coatings and sealers. Committee members suggested volunteer workdays could help with maintenance. No formal action was taken but staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan Erdman (2625 Northwestern Avenue) presented version 7 of the kayak launch signs for the Plover River water trail. Signs will be installed at launches 3 (Highway J), 4 (Ross Avenue), and 5 (Yellow Banks) in Weston, plus locations in Schofield. The Highway J launch will likely be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Marathon County Parks Foundation provided funding through state grants. Motion to approve installation passed unanimously.

Staff requested committee interest in food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds. An outside group offered to provide signs encouraging keeping food at picnic tables rather than on play equipment. The committee agreed to bring it back for further discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler consultants.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy (6504 [street inaudible]) spoke in person against allowing dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs belong in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [street] Weston), calling in under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the $8,700 figure 'too coincidental' and asking where the $10,000 grant money and Glow event funds are. He stated the Friends of Kennedy Park numbers are 'convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Defer Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion to June meeting
Approved installation of Yellow Banks kayak launch signs at three Weston locations
Staff to investigate anti-vandalism coatings and cleaning solutions for Oaklair River Trail
Bring food allergy awareness signage proposal back for future discussion
Begin Mock Miller Park master plan process with Rettler kickoff meeting
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April budget and financial reports. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam about the clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and budget. Committee member Evan announced his resignation as he is moving away from Weston.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Staff remarks and farmers market discussion

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion carried with no opposition.

discussions.item: TIF one and TIF two detail reports
discussions.body: A committee member asked about the timeline for TIF closure. Staff explained they are anticipating October-November for calling the bonds, which are due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. Staff confirmed the numbers still look good and they will be able to pay off the debt.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried with no opposition.

Clerk Pam presented extensively on the clerk's office operations covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and staffing. She noted the office went from four full-time staff to currently two full-time and one part-time, with plans to add another part-time person. She highlighted that 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is processed through the clerk's office walk-ins. Discussion occurred about pet licensing fees increasing from $10 to $20 due to Marathon County raising fees to cover Humane Society costs. Committee members discussed the $30,000 annual cost of the quarterly newsletter 'The Wire' and whether alternatives like email distribution could reduce costs, but noted previous attempts to eliminate mailing were met with resident pushback.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well, with her attending the last three Saturdays from 6 AM for 6-7 hours. The market has expanded to include coffee vendors, new food trucks, and craft vendors. Produce vendors will increase mid to late June. Committee members raised concerns about parking on Alderson and Newport streets near the market, with staff clarifying that parking is only allowed on the west side of Alderson.

Evan announced he will be stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. He thanked the committee for his time serving. The chair noted there are now open spots on this and other committees and encouraged members to reach out to citizens who may be interested in participating.

publicComment: No public comment was offered. The chair asked for public comments from the audience and online, and none were received.
actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIF bonds, likely this summer, with bonds due October 1st
Staff to continue working with Ellers regarding bond payoff process
Clerk's office seeking to hire one additional part-time employee
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee spots
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met on May 4th to address several regulatory matters including amendments to nuisance ordinances, e-bike regulations, overnight parking rules, and building demolition orders. Key actions included recommending approval of Chapter 50 nuisance amendments, an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspections, address corrections, and a raze order for a condemned property on Shore Avenue.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Overnight parking regulations October 31 to May 1
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Jim Senalt spoke critically about the new farmers market location, expressing concerns that parking vehicles on grass violates village code and will destroy the park. He questioned ADA compliance for handicap parking on grass rather than asphalt, asked about the rationale for adding fire inspections for mobile food vendors, and criticized the lack of clarity in food truck regulations. He also noted that some code violation cases have been pending for years without visible progress.

Roman/Audrey reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sale was completed and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls for March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The police department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th starting in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work with zoning during summer handling violations and pet licensing. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including previously discussed changes to tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet switched) and language requiring compliance with jurisdiction authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Committee discussed the proposed e-bike ordinance coordinated with other municipalities. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles) from roadways, sidewalks, and trails, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion addressed whether electric scooters used by middle schoolers should be exempted, with Chief Hunt suggesting officers use discretion. The committee decided to leave scooter language as-is and rely on officer judgment. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with emotos prohibited on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously.

Brent Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction window (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (most use 5 hours or less, typically 2am-7am). Michael from Public Works noted the even/odd system was adopted because snow plowing rarely occurs before 4-5am. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight parking entirely, preferring uniformity with other municipalities. Committee agreed to table the item until July while Michael identifies specific problem areas that need signage.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their property and DC Everest parking lot where parishioners park. Staff estimated a basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000, while a lighted system would cost $25,000-30,000. Committee expressed reluctance to set precedent of paying for crosswalks on request. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend board approval if Mount Olive agrees to cover installation costs passed unanimously. Michael will contact Mount Olive about cost sharing.

Chief Hunt explained a new regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston. Food trucks inspected by any participating department receive a sticker valid across all jurisdictions, eliminating duplicate inspections. Fire Marshal Marty already conducted one inspection this week that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Campkey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections needed for E911 compliance: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence near an apartment building, and 2211 Radkey Avenue for a new duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt mentioned an additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski where eight apartments have eight separate addresses plus a utility room address. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on a structure abandoned since 2010-2011 that has been posted since 2021. The fire department previously declined to do a training burn due to unsafe conditions. The property was discovered to have chickens housed inside when someone reported lights on in the structure. Current owners are up to date on taxes and prefer the village handle demolition with costs added to their tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment, criticizing the new farmers market location as 'terrible execution' that will destroy park grass, questioning ADA compliance for handicap parking placement on grass, asking about the origin of food truck fire inspections, noting ambiguity in food vendor regulations, and calling for better accountability on long-pending code enforcement cases.
actionItems: Chapter 50 nuisance amendments recommended for Board of Trustees approval
E-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on public property recommended for Board approval
Overnight parking regulations tabled until July; Michael to identify specific problem areas for signage
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church about paying for crosswalk installation costs
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections approved
Address corrections for 5915 Delicoski, Ross Avenue properties, and 2211 Radkey Avenue recommended for approval
Staff to investigate Weston Villa Apartments addressing issue
Raze order authorized for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property condition at Sternberg and South Timber
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 6th at 5:00 p.m.

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
